failure_modes.text: A premortem becomes superficial when it produces an obvious worry list without owners, mitigations, or decision changes.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the exercise produces an obvious list of worries without owners, mitigations, or decision changes tied to what was surfaced.
failure_modes.mitigation: Tie each surfaced risk to a named owner and a concrete plan change; if no risk alters the plan, the exercise was performative.

failure_modes.text: Teams can substitute a shallow pro-con exercise for the harder work of imagining systemic failure paths.
failure_modes.source_quote: A common anti-pattern is substituting the deep, critical work of a Premortem with a simpler, more available tool, such as a basic pro-con list.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use the dialectic standard: every failure hypothesis must be met with a direct challenge before synthesis, not just listed alongside positives.

failure_modes.text: Mental fatigue can make the exercise lazy, obvious, and non-imaginative because participants default to fast thinking instead of deeper failure exploration.
failure_modes.source_quote: If participants are already mentally depleted, they may rely on the fast, intuitive System 1 thinking ... resulting in a superficial analysis.
failure_modes.mitigation: Simulate extremes and outside perspectives to force novel failure paths beyond the obvious; stress-test edge cases that depleted participants would skip.

premortem_questions.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.text: Do we still remember the question we are trying to answer, or have we substituted an easier one?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Do we still remember the question we are trying to answer? Or have we substituted an easier one?

premortem_questions.text: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.text: Assume the project failed and work backward from that future state to surface the weak links that could have caused it.
heuristics.source_quote: Before the project begins, the team gathers and assumes that the project has failed spectacularly. They then spend time detailing the plausible reasons why it failed.

heuristics.text: Expose second-order consequences and domino effects instead of evaluating only the first visible step.
heuristics.source_quote: The application of the Premortem requires participants to engage in second-order thinking.

heuristics.text: Tie each surfaced failure path to an owner, mitigation, or reversal threshold so the exercise changes the plan instead of decorating it.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when the exercise produces an obvious list of worries without owners, mitigations, or decision changes tied to what was surfaced.

curation_notes.summary: The source is naturally chunk-ready on backward failure simulation, anti-optimism discipline, and forcing failure paths into binding mitigations.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor risk-workshop sprawl that widened beyond the raw source's stronger backward-failure and anti-superficiality material.
open_questions: The third heuristic deliberately reuses the danger passage as an operational rule because the source makes the keep/drop boundary unusually clear there.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on systems thinking, second-order-thinking, and checklist-like risk surfacing for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is strongest on confirmation-bias, commitment-bias, and conjunctive overoptimism pressures.
structured_tensions: The source explicitly names premortem conflict with commitment-bias and carries clean current-vocabulary pressure against confirmation-biased plan defense.
